words.definition: all the subjects and content taught in a school or programme
example_sentences: The school updated its curriculum to include coding.
The history curriculum covers events from 1700 to present.
A broad curriculum prepares students for many careers.
words.pronunciation_tip: kuh-RIK-yoo-lum — four syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the programme of studies' — say 'the curriculum'.

words.definition: a long piece of research written for a university degree
example_sentences: She spent a year writing her dissertation on urban poverty.
His dissertation was awarded the highest grade.
A dissertation is usually 10,000 to 15,000 words.
words.pronunciation_tip: diss-er-TAY-shun — four syllables, stress on third.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'my long essay for university' — say 'my dissertation'.

words.definition: copying someone else's work and presenting it as your own
example_sentences: He was caught for plagiarism and failed the course.
Plagiarism is treated very seriously in universities.
Using AI to write your essay without declaring it may count as plagiarism.
words.pronunciation_tip: PLAY-juh-riz-um — four syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'copying' only — say 'plagiarism' in academic contexts.

words.definition: the ability to read and write
example_sentences: Literacy rates have improved significantly in recent decades.
Financial literacy is increasingly important for young people.
The programme aims to improve literacy in rural communities.
words.pronunciation_tip: LIT-er-uh-see — four syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the ability to read' — say 'literacy'.

words.definition: teaching, especially individual or private teaching
example_sentences: She gives private tuition in maths to school students.
Tuition fees at universities have risen sharply.
He improved dramatically with one-to-one tuition.
words.pronunciation_tip: tyoo-ISH-un — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'teaching fee' only — 'tuition' also means the teaching itself.

words.definition: to officially register on a course or at an institution
example_sentences: She enrolled in a French evening class.
He enrolled at university in September.
You can enrol online or in person at the main office.
words.pronunciation_tip: en-ROHL — stress on second syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I registered in the course' — say 'I enrolled in the course'.

words.definition: influence from people of your own age or social group
example_sentences: Peer pressure can lead young people to make bad decisions.
She resisted peer pressure and made her own choices.
Peer pressure is strongest during teenage years.
words.pronunciation_tip: PEER PRESH-er.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'pressure from friends' — say 'peer pressure'.
words.collocations: ['resist peer pressure', 'give in to peer pressure', 'peer pressure to']

words.definition: a list of topics covered in a course or exam
example_sentences: The syllabus covers grammar, vocabulary, and writing skills.
Check the syllabus before the exam to know what's included.
She designed the new syllabus herself.
words.pronunciation_tip: SIL-uh-bus — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't confuse syllabus (one course's content list) with curriculum (all subjects).

words.definition: money given to a student to help pay for their education
example_sentences: She won a scholarship to study at Oxford.
He received a full scholarship covering all tuition fees.
Many students rely on scholarships to fund their studies.
words.pronunciation_tip: SKOL-er-ship — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I got money for my studies' — say 'I was awarded a scholarship'.
words.collocations: ['win a scholarship', 'full scholarship', 'apply for a scholarship']

words.definition: a period of work experience, often unpaid, as part of a course
example_sentences: She did an internship at a law firm during her second year.
Internships are essential for building your CV.
He turned his internship into a full-time job.
words.pronunciation_tip: IN-turn-ship — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'work experience placement' every time — say 'internship'.

words.definition: to evaluate or measure someone's ability or knowledge
example_sentences: The teacher assessed students through regular short tests.
The exam assesses reading, writing, and listening.
Students are assessed on both process and final outcome.
words.pronunciation_tip: uh-SESS — two syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'test' and 'assess' interchangeably — assess is broader.

words.definition: the act of being present at a place regularly
example_sentences: Poor attendance will affect your final grade.
Attendance at lectures is not compulsory but is recommended.
She had perfect attendance throughout the year.
words.pronunciation_tip: uh-TEN-dunss — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'coming to school regularly' as a noun — say 'attendance'.

words.definition: to give someone a reason and enthusiasm to do something
example_sentences: The teacher motivated students by making lessons engaging.
It's hard to motivate yourself when you're exhausted.
Positive feedback motivates people far more than criticism.
words.pronunciation_tip: MOH-tih-vayt — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'make people want to do something' — say 'motivate'.

words.definition: activities done outside the regular school or university curriculum
example_sentences: She is involved in several extracurricular activities.
Sports, drama, and music are all extracurricular.
Extracurricular involvement looks good on a CV.
words.pronunciation_tip: ek-struh-kuh-RIK-yoo-ler — six syllables, stress on fourth.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'activities outside class' every time — say 'extracurricular activities'.

words.definition: the latest time by which work must be submitted
example_sentences: The deadline for the essay is next Friday at noon.
She always submits work well before the deadline.
Missing deadlines can result in a grade penalty.
words.pronunciation_tip: DED-lyn — stress on first syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'last day to give the work' — say 'the deadline'.

words.definition: the process of reviewing and studying material before an exam
example_sentences: She spent the whole week on exam revision.
Effective revision involves practising past papers.
He left his revision too late and struggled in the exam.
words.pronunciation_tip: rih-VIZH-un — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'studying for the exam' — say 'doing revision' in British English.

words.definition: the ability to analyse information and form your own conclusions
example_sentences: University education is supposed to develop critical thinking.
Critical thinking is one of the most valued graduate skills.
The course encourages critical thinking rather than memorisation.
words.pronunciation_tip: KRIT-ih-kul THINK-ing.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'thinking about things carefully' — say 'critical thinking' in academic contexts.
words.collocations: ['develop critical thinking', 'critical thinking skills', 'encourage critical thinking']

words.definition: a group of students who start a course at the same time
example_sentences: Our cohort of 30 students started together in September.
The graduation cohort was the largest in the university's history.
She stayed in touch with several people from her cohort.
words.pronunciation_tip: KOH-hawt — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'my class' in higher education — say 'my cohort'.

words.definition: information given to someone about how they can improve
example_sentences: The teacher gave detailed feedback on every essay.
Constructive feedback is more useful than just a grade.
She asked for feedback after her presentation.
words.pronunciation_tip: FEED-bak — stress on first syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the teacher told me what to improve' — say 'the teacher gave me feedback'.

words.definition: to successfully complete a degree or school qualification
example_sentences: She graduated from Tel Aviv University in 2020.
Recent graduates often struggle to find their first job.
He graduated with a first-class degree in economics.
words.pronunciation_tip: GRAD-yoo-ayt (verb) / GRAD-yoo-ut (noun) — stress on first syllable for both.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I finished university' — say 'I graduated from university'.
